content.text: An App Registration is the global definition of an application in Entra ID — it is created once in the home tenant and contains the app's identity (client ID), authentication settings, requested permissions, and credential configuration. When users or admins consent to the app in their tenant, Entra creates a Service Principal (also called an Enterprise Application) — a local policy object that represents the app within that specific tenant.

The distinction matters: App Registrations live in one place (the developer's home tenant), while Service Principals can exist in thousands of tenants. Configuration changes to the App Registration (adding a redirect URI, changing scopes) affect the definition; changes to the Service Principal (consent, role assignments, Conditional Access) affect behavior in a specific tenant.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing the App Registration as the global definition in the home tenant, creating a Service Principal there, while multi-tenant consent creates Service Principals in other tenants — all sharing the same appId

content.text: App registrations are created programmatically via Microsoft Graph or manually in the Entra admin center. The `signInAudience` property controls which account types can sign in — single-tenant (`AzureADMyOrg`) is the most secure default and should be used unless the app genuinely needs to serve users from other tenants or personal Microsoft accounts.

# Create an app registration via Microsoft Graph PowerShell
# Requires: Application.ReadWrite.All

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Application.ReadWrite.All"

# Create a web app registration (Authorization Code flow)
$appParams = @{
    displayName              = "My Web App"
    signInAudience           = "AzureADMyOrg"  # single-tenant
    # signInAudience options:
    # AzureADMyOrg                  – single tenant (most secure)
    # AzureADMultipleOrgs           – any Entra tenant
    # AzureADandPersonalMicrosoftAccount – Entra + personal MSA
    # PersonalMicrosoftAccount      – personal MSA only

    web = @{
        redirectUris = @(
            "https://myapp.example.com/callback",
            "http://localhost:5000/callback"  # dev only
        )
        implicitGrantSettings = @{
            enableIdTokenIssuance     = $false  # Use Auth Code flow, not implicit
            enableAccessTokenIssuance = $false
        }
    }

    # Request Microsoft Graph permissions
    requiredResourceAccess = @(
        @{
            resourceAppId = "00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000"  # Microsoft Graph
            resourceAccess = @(
                @{ id = "e1fe6dd8-ba31-4d61-89e7-88639da4683d"; type = "Scope" }  # User.Read
                @{ id = "37f7f235-527c-4136-accd-4a02d197296e"; type = "Scope" }  # openid
                @{ id = "14dad69e-099b-42c9-810b-d002981feec1"; type = "Scope" }  # profile
            )
        }
    )
}

$app = New-MgApplication @appParams
Write-Host "Created app: $($app.DisplayName)"
Write-Host "App ID (client_id): $($app.AppId)"
Write-Host "Object ID: $($app.Id)"

# Create the corresponding service principal
$sp = New-MgServicePrincipal -AppId $app.AppId
Write-Host "Service Principal ID: $($sp.Id)"
Users in the home tenant only
Consumer-facing apps with work/personal account support
Consumer apps with no enterprise requirement
Always start with AzureADMyOrg unless you have a specific reason to support other tenants. Broader signInAudience widens the attack surface and requires more careful multi-tenant token validation.

Confidential client applications (web apps, daemons) prove their identity to Entra using either a client secret (shared password) or a certificate (asymmetric key). Certificates are strongly preferred in production — they cannot be accidentally logged or included in source code, and they survive credential rotation without an application restart if the private key is in Key Vault.

# Add a client secret to the app registration
# Secrets are for confidential clients (web apps with a server-side component)
# Do NOT add secrets to public clients (SPAs, mobile/desktop)

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Application.ReadWrite.All"
$appId = "<your-app-object-id>"  # Object ID, NOT client_id

# Add a client secret (expires in 1 year)
$secretParams = @{
    passwordCredential = @{
        displayName = "MyApp-Secret-$(Get-Date -f 'yyyyMM')"
        endDateTime = (Get-Date).AddYears(1)
    }
}
$secret = Add-MgApplicationPassword -ApplicationId $appId @secretParams
Write-Host "Secret value (copy now — not shown again): $($secret.SecretText)"
Write-Host "Secret ID: $($secret.KeyId)"
Write-Host "Expires: $($secret.EndDateTime)"

# BETTER: Add a certificate credential instead of a secret
# Generate a self-signed cert (dev) or use one from Key Vault (prod)
$cert = New-SelfSignedCertificate \
    -Subject 'CN=MyApp' \
    -CertStoreLocation 'Cert:\CurrentUser\My' \
    -KeyExportPolicy Exportable \
    -NotAfter (Get-Date).AddYears(2)

$certBytes = $cert.Export([System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509ContentType]::Cert)
$keyCredential = @{
    type        = "AsymmetricX509Cert"
    usage       = "Verify"
    key         = $certBytes
    displayName = "MyApp-Cert-$(Get-Date -f 'yyyyMM')"
    endDateTime = $cert.NotAfter
}
Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId $appId \
    -KeyCredentials @($keyCredential)
Write-Host "Certificate thumbprint registered: $($cert.Thumbprint)"

# In production: store the private key in Azure Key Vault and reference it
# from your app using Managed Identity — no certificate file on disk
Never store client secrets in source code, config files checked into git, or application logs
Rotate secrets before they expire — Entra shows the expiry date; set a calendar reminder or use Azure Policy to alert on near-expiry secrets
Use Managed Identity for apps running on Azure (App Service, VMs, AKS) — Managed Identity eliminates client secrets entirely for Azure-hosted workloads
Limit the number of active secrets to 2 maximum (one current, one next) — more than that suggests rotation is not happening
Certificate private keys in Azure Key Vault can be accessed by the application via Managed Identity, keeping the private key out of the application process entirely

When you build an API that other applications call, you define OAuth 2.0 scopes (also called delegated permissions or Oauth2PermissionScopes) on your API's app registration. These scopes appear on the consent prompt when a user authorizes a client app to call your API on their behalf. Client apps then include your API's scope in their token requests.

# Define custom OAuth 2.0 scopes for your API (Expose an API section)
# This allows other apps to request permission to call your API

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Application.ReadWrite.All"
$apiAppId = "<your-api-app-object-id>"

# Step 1: Set the App ID URI (the 'api://' prefix is conventional)
$apiApp = Get-MgApplication -ApplicationId $apiAppId
$appClientId = $apiApp.AppId

Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId $apiAppId \
    -IdentifierUris @("api://$appClientId")

# Step 2: Add a scope (Oauth2PermissionScope)
$scopeId = [System.Guid]::NewGuid().ToString()
$scope = @{
    id                      = $scopeId
    value                   = "Data.Read"           # the scope name clients request
    adminConsentDisplayName = "Read data from MyAPI"
    adminConsentDescription = "Allows the app to read data from MyAPI on behalf of the signed-in user."
    userConsentDisplayName  = "Read your data from MyAPI"
    userConsentDescription  = "Allows this app to read your data from MyAPI."
    isEnabled               = $true
    type                    = "User"  # 'User' = user can consent; 'Admin' = admin-only
}

# Get current scopes and append the new one
$api = $apiApp.Api
$api.Oauth2PermissionScopes += $scope
Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId $apiAppId -Api $api

# Step 3: In the CLIENT app, add the API permission:
$clientAppId = "<client-app-object-id>"
$apiSpId = (Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$appClientId'").Id
$apiSp   = Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $apiSpId
$scopeObj = $apiSp.Oauth2PermissionScopes | Where-Object Value -eq 'Data.Read'

Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId $clientAppId \
    -RequiredResourceAccess @(
        @{
            resourceAppId  = $appClientId
            resourceAccess = @(
                @{ id = $scopeObj.Id; type = "Scope" }
            )
        }
    )
# Clients now request: scope = 'api://<api-client-id>/Data.Read'
The App ID URI (identifier URI) is the audience (`aud`) for tokens issued to your API — clients request tokens with `scope='api://<client-id>/Data.Read'`
Use `type='Admin'` for scopes that should require admin consent — operations like reading all users' data or sending mail as any user
Use `type='User'` for scopes that are safe for users to grant themselves — reading their own profile or calendar
Pre-authorize trusted client apps in the 'Authorized client applications' section to allow silent token acquisition without prompting the user

App Roles define the authorization model for your application. Unlike scopes (which are delegated — a user grants them), app roles can be assigned to users, groups, or other applications. The assigned roles appear in the `roles` claim of the JWT — your API reads this claim to decide what the caller is permitted to do.

# Define App Roles for authorization in your API
# App roles appear in the 'roles' claim of both delegated and app-only tokens

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Application.ReadWrite.All", "AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All"
$apiAppId = "<your-api-app-object-id>"

# Add two app roles: Reader and Writer
$readerRoleId = [System.Guid]::NewGuid().ToString()
$writerRoleId = [System.Guid]::NewGuid().ToString()

$appRoles = @(
    @{
        id                  = $readerRoleId
        displayName         = "Data Reader"
        description         = "Can read data from the API"
        value               = "Data.Read"       # appears in 'roles' claim
        allowedMemberTypes  = @("User", "Application")  # both users and app-only
        isEnabled           = $true
    },
    @{
        id                  = $writerRoleId
        displayName         = "Data Writer"
        description         = "Can write data to the API"
        value               = "Data.Write"
        allowedMemberTypes  = @("User", "Application")
        isEnabled           = $true
    }
)
Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId $apiAppId -AppRoles $appRoles

# Assign a user to the Reader role:
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$($(Get-MgApplication -ApplicationId $apiAppId).AppId)'"
$user = Get-MgUser -UserId alice@contoso.com

New-MgUserAppRoleAssignment \
    -UserId        $user.Id \
    -PrincipalId   $user.Id \
    -ResourceId    $sp.Id \
    -AppRoleId     $readerRoleId

# Assign a daemon app to the Writer role:
$daemonSp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "displayName eq 'MyDaemonApp'"
New-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment \
    -ServicePrincipalId $daemonSp.Id \
    -PrincipalId        $daemonSp.Id \
    -ResourceId         $sp.Id \
    -AppRoleId          $writerRoleId

# In your API, check the 'roles' claim:
# [Authorize(Roles = "Data.Write")]
# Or: User.IsInRole("Data.Read")

For RBAC in your API, prefer App Roles over groups — roles are declared per-app and have descriptive values ('Data.Read') while groups carry OIDs that require a lookup. App roles also avoid the 150-group claim limit.

When a user or admin first encounters an app's permission request, Entra presents a consent prompt. Users can consent to delegated permissions marked `type='User'`; admin-only permissions and Application permissions always require an admin to consent. Admin consent can be granted tenant-wide, bypassing individual user prompts for all employees.

Organizations typically pre-grant admin consent for approved internal apps (no friction for users) while requiring user consent review for third-party apps through the Admin Consent Request workflow. Auditing what has been consented to is a critical security practice — over-consented apps with broad delegated permissions are a common lateral movement vector.

# Manage app consent via Microsoft Graph
# Admin consent allows all users in the tenant to use the app without individual prompts

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "DelegatedPermissionGrant.ReadWrite.All", "AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All"

# Grant admin consent for delegated permissions (users can sign in without consent prompt)
$sp  = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '<your-client-id>'"
$graph = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'"

# Check existing consent grants
$grants = Get-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -Filter "clientId eq '$($sp.Id)'"
$grants | Select-Object scope, consentType, principalId

# Grant admin consent for all users (consentType = AllPrincipals)
$consentParams = @{
    clientId    = $sp.Id          # the app's service principal
    resourceId  = $graph.Id       # Microsoft Graph service principal
    scope       = "User.Read profile openid email offline_access"  # scopes to consent
    consentType = "AllPrincipals"  # tenant-wide; use 'Principal' for per-user
}
New-MgOauth2PermissionGrant @consentParams

# View all apps with tenant-wide consent (audit what's been consented)
$allGrants = Get-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -Filter "consentType eq 'AllPrincipals'" -All
foreach ($g in $allGrants) {
    $client = Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $g.ClientId
    $resource = Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $g.ResourceId
    Write-Host "$($client.DisplayName) → $($resource.DisplayName): $($g.Scope)"
}

# Revoke consent (e.g., when decommissioning an app):
$grantToRevoke = $allGrants | Where-Object { $_.ClientId -eq $sp.Id }
$grantToRevoke | ForEach-Object {
    Remove-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -OAuth2PermissionGrantId $_.Id
}
Admin on behalf of a specific user
First-party clients calling your own API

Audit OAuth 2.0 permission grants quarterly. Remove consent for apps that are no longer used or that have accumulated permissions beyond what they need. A single over-privileged consented app can be as damaging as a compromised admin account.

App registrations accumulate over time — development apps, test apps, and forgotten integrations add up. Unmanaged registrations with valid credentials and broad permissions represent real security risk. Build lifecycle discipline: assign owners, monitor credential expiry, and audit for stale apps regularly.

# App Registration lifecycle management
# Owners, expiry, stale app audit, and deletion

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Application.ReadWrite.All", "Application.Read.All"

# Add an owner to the app registration
# Owners can manage the registration without being Global Admins
$appObjectId = "<app-object-id>"
$owner = Get-MgUser -UserId alice@contoso.com
New-MgApplicationOwnerByRef -ApplicationId $appObjectId \
    -OdataId "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directoryObjects/$($owner.Id)"

# Audit: find apps with expiring secrets or certificates
$apps = Get-MgApplication -All -Property "displayName,id,appId,passwordCredentials,keyCredentials"
$soon = (Get-Date).AddDays(30)

foreach ($a in $apps) {
    $expiringSec  = $a.PasswordCredentials | Where-Object { $_.EndDateTime -lt $soon }
    $expiringCert = $a.KeyCredentials      | Where-Object { $_.EndDateTime -lt $soon }

    if ($expiringSec -or $expiringCert) {
        Write-Warning "App '$($a.DisplayName)' has credentials expiring within 30 days!"
        $expiringSec  | Select-Object @{N="Type";E={"Secret"}}, DisplayName, EndDateTime
        $expiringCert | Select-Object @{N="Type";E={"Cert"}},   DisplayName, EndDateTime
    }
}

# Audit: find stale app registrations (no sign-ins in 90 days)
# Cross-reference sign-in logs with app registration list:
$cutoff = (Get-Date).AddDays(-90).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
$recentSignIns = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri ("https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/signIns" +
          "?" + '$select=appId&' + '$filter=' +
          [System.Uri]::EscapeDataString("createdDateTime ge $cutoff") +
          "&" + '$top=999') |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

$activeAppIds = $recentSignIns.appId | Sort-Object -Unique
$staleApps = $apps | Where-Object { $_.AppId -notin $activeAppIds }
Write-Host "$($staleApps.Count) app registrations with no sign-ins in 90 days (review for deletion)"

# Soft-delete an app (goes to the deleted items recycle bin for 30 days):
# Remove-MgApplication -ApplicationId $appObjectId
# Restore from recycle bin within 30 days:
# Restore-MgDirectoryDeletedItem -DirectoryObjectId $appObjectId
Every app registration should have at least one non-admin owner — the owning team is responsible for rotating credentials and decommissioning when done
Set credential expiry to a maximum of 1 year for secrets, 2 years for certificates — longer lifetimes compound the risk of undetected credential leaks
Use Azure Policy or a scheduled runbook to alert on secrets/certificates expiring within 30 days
Apps with no sign-ins in 90 days should be reviewed: decommission dead apps, add an expiry date to development apps
When deleting, remove consent grants and app role assignments first, then delete the registration — the deleted app goes to the recycle bin for 30 days before permanent deletion

The application manifest is the underlying JSON representation of the App Registration object. The Entra admin center exposes a subset of the manifest through its UI; the full manifest is accessible via the Microsoft Graph Applications API. Some important settings are only available via the manifest, including the access token version, implicit grant settings, and group membership claims.

# The app registration manifest is the underlying JSON object
# Fetch and update via Graph — useful for bulk or programmatic configuration

import requests, json

APP_OBJECT_ID  = "<app-object-id>"
ACCESS_TOKEN   = "<admin-access-token>"  # Application.ReadWrite.All
HEADERS = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {ACCESS_TOKEN}', 'Content-Type': 'application/json'}

# Fetch the current manifest
app = requests.get(
    f'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/applications/{APP_OBJECT_ID}',
    headers=HEADERS
).json()

print('Current signInAudience:', app['signInAudience'])
print('Current redirect URIs: ', app.get('web', {}).get('redirectUris', []))
print('Existing app roles:    ', len(app.get('appRoles', [])))

# Common manifest settings and what they control:
# app['accessTokenAcceptedVersion'] = 2    -- emit v2 tokens (required for OIDC, recommended)
# app['oauth2AllowIdTokenImplicitFlow'] = False  -- disable implicit grant (recommended)
# app['oauth2AllowImplicitFlow']        = False  -- disable implicit access token grant
# app['optionalClaims']                         -- extra claims in tokens
# app['publicClient']['redirectUris']           -- native/mobile redirect URIs
# app['spa']['redirectUris']                    -- SPA redirect URIs (uses PKCE)
# app['groupMembershipClaims']          = 'SecurityGroup'  -- include groups in tokens

# Update: enable v2 token format and disable implicit grant
patch = {
    'accessTokenAcceptedVersion': 2,
    'oauth2AllowIdTokenImplicitFlow': False,
    'oauth2AllowImplicitFlow': False,
}
resp = requests.patch(
    f'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/applications/{APP_OBJECT_ID}',
    headers=HEADERS,
    json=patch
)
print('Updated:', resp.status_code)  # 204 No Content on success

# Verify the change
updated = requests.get(
    f'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/applications/{APP_OBJECT_ID}',
    headers=HEADERS
).json()
print('Token version:', updated.get('accessTokenAcceptedVersion'))
Set `accessTokenAcceptedVersion: 2` for all new apps — v2 tokens use the modern issuer format and include `oid`, `tid`, and other standard claims
Set `oauth2AllowImplicitFlow: false` and `oauth2AllowIdTokenImplicitFlow: false` to disable the deprecated implicit grant
Use `spa.redirectUris` for single-page apps (not `web.redirectUris`) — SPA redirect URIs use Auth Code + PKCE automatically and do not require a client secret
The `groupMembershipClaims` setting controls which groups appear in tokens: `None`, `SecurityGroup` (OIDs only), or `All` (security + distribution lists + roles)

App Registrations are the global identity definition for applications in Entra ID. Each registration has a unique client ID, configures supported authentication flows and redirect URIs, defines required permissions (delegated and application), exposes its own API scopes, and holds credential configuration (secrets or certificates).
App Registration = global definition (one per app); Service Principal = per-tenant policy instance (one per tenant that consents)
Use single-tenant signInAudience by default; only broaden if your app genuinely serves users from other tenants
Prefer certificate credentials over client secrets for confidential clients; use Managed Identity for Azure-hosted apps
Define OAuth 2.0 scopes to let other apps request delegated access to your API
Use App Roles for authorization — they appear in the 'roles' claim and avoid the 150-group limit
